methodologies.name: Crawl-Walk-Run Automation Gating
methodologies.summary: Roll an agent out in three stages, with a clear gate between each one. In the first stage the agent only suggests, and a person acts. In the second stage the agent acts on internal staff, who can fix mistakes. In the third stage the agent acts directly on outside customers. Each stage is set per action type, not for the whole agent. The same agent can be in the last stage for safe read-only actions and the first stage for refunds. To move up a stage, an action type must clear a published metric bar. If its numbers drop, it moves back down on its own.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Crawl: every action type starts here
  Crawl --> Walk: clears acceptance-rate bar for hold duration
  Walk --> Run: clears customer-outcome bar for hold duration
  Walk --> Crawl: regression below Crawl-Walk bar (with hysteresis)
  Run --> Walk: regression below Walk-Run customer-outcome bar
  Run --> Crawl: severe regression / incident-driven demote
  note right of Crawl
    Agent emits suggestions only
    Human accepts / rejects / edits
  end note
  note right of Walk
    Agent acts on internal staff
    Internal staff can correct
  end note
  note right of Run
    Agent acts on external customers
    Customer-outcome metric monitored
  end note

methodologies.example_scenario:
A mid-size retailer's customer-support team is deploying an AI agent that can answer questions, look up orders, and issue refunds. They enumerate four action types up front: 'reply to FAQ-style ticket', 'lookup order status' (read-only), 'issue refund up to $50', 'issue refund above $50'. They publish per-tier bars: Crawl-Walk requires 90% human acceptance over 200 actions in a two-week window; Walk-Run requires a customer-outcome metric (re-open rate within 7 days < 4%) over 500 actions in three weeks.

On day one every action type sits at Crawl. The agent drafts; humans accept, edit, or reject. After two weeks, FAQ replies hit 94% acceptance and lookup hits 99% — both promote to Walk. Lookup also clears the customer-outcome bar within a further three weeks (re-open rate flat) and promotes to Run. FAQ replies sit at Walk longer because the re-open rate creeps up to 5% on edge cases involving order modifications; the action type stays at Walk pending fixes.

The refund actions move separately. Refund-up-to-$50 reaches Walk after a month. Refund-above-$50 stays at Crawl indefinitely by team policy, regardless of acceptance, because the blast radius justifies a permanent human-in-the-loop. Six weeks in, a prompt change inadvertently regressed FAQ acceptance to 81%; the system auto-demoted FAQ back to Crawl, the team rolled back the prompt, and a week later FAQ returned to Walk. Nobody framed the demotion as a failure — it was the gate doing exactly what it was designed to do.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The three tiers are **Crawl** (agent emits suggestions only; a human accepts, rejects, or edits each one), **Walk** (agent acts directly on internal staff, who can catch and correct mistakes), and **Run** (agent acts directly on external customers, monitored by a customer-outcome metric).

- **Tier is per-action-type, not per-agent.** Enumerate discrete action types (e.g. `reply_to_ticket`, `issue_refund_le_50`, `issue_refund_gt_50`, `escalate`) and assign each its own current tier. A single deployed agent can simultaneously be at Run for read-only lookups and Crawl for high-value refunds.
- **Promotion gates carry both a metric bar and a hold duration.** Crawl→Walk is typically gated on a human-acceptance rate over N actions within a window. Walk→Run requires a *different*, customer-facing outcome metric (re-open rate, refund-reversal rate, message-flag rate) — internal acceptance does not imply external success.
- **Regression is automatic and hysteretic.** If a tier's live metric falls below its bar, the action type auto-demotes one step. A hysteresis margin (demote below the bar, re-promote only above bar + ε, held for the duration) prevents flapping around the threshold.
- **Policy can pin a tier.** High-blast-radius action types may be held at Crawl indefinitely by explicit policy regardless of metrics, keeping a permanent human-in-the-loop where no reliable undo exists.

The core move is decoupling *capability* (the agent can plausibly perform the action) from *autonomy* (the agent is permitted to perform it unsupervised). Capability is necessary but never sufficient; the data clearing the gate is what grants autonomy.
methodologies.intent: Separate what an agent can do from what it is allowed to do on its own. A system that could plausibly act gets to act only after the data earns it, one action type at a time.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this for any agent that could plausibly act on its own in ways customers feel. Examples are replying to tickets, refunding orders, sending outbound messages, or changing production resources. The right fit is when one bad action does real harm and there is no reliable way to undo it. Don't apply it for read-only or sandboxed agents, where a bad action causes no harm.

inputs.name: Catalog of action types
inputs.description: A list of every distinct action the agent can take. Each one has its own level of risk if it goes wrong.

inputs.name: Promotion bar per tier
inputs.description: Published targets an action type must hit to move up a stage. These cover human acceptance, internal completion, and customer outcomes.

inputs.name: Per-action-type metric pipeline
inputs.description: Logging that records, for every action, which stage it ran at and what happened next.

outputs.name: Per-action-type tier assignment
outputs.description: A live map from each action type to its current stage (Crawl, Walk, or Run).

outputs.name: Promotion log
outputs.description: A record you can audit. It shows which action types moved up or down, when, and on what evidence.

steps.name: Enumerate action types
steps.description: List every distinct action the agent can take. 'Reply to ticket', 'issue refund up to $50', 'issue refund above $50', and 'escalate to human' are four separate types, not one.

steps.name: Publish the metric bar per tier
steps.description: For each stage, write down the metric and the target an action type must hit to move up. Use acceptance rate, completion rate, or customer outcome. Also set how long each stage must hold before you consider promotion.

steps.name: Start every action type at Crawl
steps.description: On day one, no action type starts in Walk or Run, no matter how it was built. In Crawl the agent only suggests. A human accepts, rejects, or edits each suggestion.

steps.name: Promote one action type at a time
steps.description: When an action type clears the Crawl-to-Walk bar, move up that one type only. The agent now acts on internal staff for that action. Everything else stays in Crawl.

steps.name: Watch for regression and auto-demote
steps.description: If a stage's metric drops below its bar, move that action type back down. Use a small margin so it does not bounce up and down. A demotion is not a failure. It is the system working as designed.

steps.name: Advance to Run with the customer-outcome metric
steps.description: Moving from Walk to Run needs more than internal acceptance. It needs a real customer outcome, such as a ticket that stays resolved, a refund that is not reversed, or a message that is not flagged. Internal acceptance is not the same as customer success.

principles: Autonomy belongs to action types, not to the agent as a whole.
Every stage publishes both a metric bar and a hold time. The hold time stops one good week from promoting a risky action.
Promote one action at a time. Demote automatically.
Internal acceptance is not customer success. The Walk-to-Run gate uses a different metric from the Crawl-to-Walk gate.

known_failure_modes.note: Shipping the agent fully autonomous to external customers from day one because internal demos looked good — behaviour that cannot be retracted.
Hiding the agent behind a human reviewer forever and never establishing the promotion bar — the agent never gets to add value.

sources.title: AI Engineering
sources.chapter: Ch 10 'AI Engineering Architecture and User Feedback'
sources.quote: Architecture Steps: Enhance Context, Put in Guardrails, Add Model Router and Gateway, Reduce Latency with Caches, Add Agent Patterns

sources.title: Building A Generative AI Platform (Chip Huyen, author's blog precursor to AI Engineering Ch 10)
sources.quote: The initial expansion of a platform usually involves adding mechanisms to allow the system to augment each query with the necessary information ... Guardrails help reduce AI risks and protect not just your users but also you, the developers ... An application can use different models to respond to different types of queries

sources.title: AI Engineering Architecture and User Feedback (Alex Strick van Linschoten)
sources.quote: User feedback systems inherently contain various biases ... negative experience bias and self-selection bias

methodologies.name: Shadow Canary Bandit Rollout
methodologies.summary: Roll out a change to an agent in stages that expose more users as confidence grows. First, run the new version next to the live one and compare them offline, so no user is affected (a shadow run). Next, send a small slice of real traffic to the new version at full risk (a canary). Last, let the system send more traffic to whichever version gets better results from real users. Each stage is a gate. If the numbers get worse at any stage, the rollout stops and the failing cases are saved for study. This works on one change at a time, such as a new prompt, model, or retrieval tweak, rather than on a whole action type.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Shadow: candidate change submitted
  Shadow --> Canary: shadow diff acceptable, no latency/error regression
  Shadow --> Halted: gross output drift or crash
  Canary --> Bandit: clears canary exit criteria on 1-5% slice
  Canary --> Halted: any exit criterion violated
  Bandit --> Promoted: posterior reward sustained above incumbent
  Bandit --> Halted: posterior reward falls below incumbent with confidence
  Halted --> Shadow: fixed build resubmitted
  Halted --> [*]: change abandoned
  Promoted --> [*]
  note right of Halted
    Regression traces captured
    Folded into eval set
  end note

methodologies.example_scenario:
A team running a high-volume customer-support chatbot has a new prompt that they believe handles refund-policy questions better. They submit it as a candidate change. Shadow stage: production traffic is mirrored to the new prompt for 48 hours; outputs are diffed offline against the incumbent. Latency comes in 6ms lower, no crashes, output drift modest and concentrated exactly on refund-policy queries (the targeted improvement). Shadow passes.

Canary stage: 2% of live traffic routes to the new prompt with the frozen exit criteria — minimum 4,000 conversations, no >0.5% absolute error-rate regression, no >5% latency p95 regression, guardrail-trip count not increasing. After 36 hours the criteria are met on every axis, including a 3-point lift on the refund-question subset. Canary passes. Allocation is handed to a Bayesian bandit with the new prompt and the incumbent as the two arms; reward is binary thumbs-up from the user. Within five days the bandit has shifted to 78% allocation to the new prompt because its posterior reward is sustained 4 points above the incumbent.

A week later, an unrelated retrieval change is canary-tested and fails — the canary error rate spiked because the retriever returned empty results on a class of queries the shadow comparison hadn't surfaced. The build is halted, the failing traces are bundled and added to the regression eval set, and the change goes back for rework. The team didn't roll back quietly — the trace bundle is exactly what's needed to write the next round of tests, so the failure produces a permanent improvement to the harness rather than just an embarrassed Slack thread.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The rollout has three escalating exposure stages, each with a frozen exit criterion set before the stage opens.

- **Shadow.** Production traffic is mirrored to the candidate build, which runs in parallel but its outputs never reach users. Outputs and metrics are diffed offline against the incumbent. This catches crashes, latency regressions, and gross output drift at zero user risk.
- **Canary.** A small live slice (typically 1-5%) is routed to the candidate at full risk. Exit criteria are concrete and frozen: minimum sample size, maximum allowed absolute error-rate regression, maximum p95 latency regression, and a non-increasing guardrail-trip count, all over a fixed window.
- **Bandit / A-B.** Once the canary clears, traffic allocation is handed to a multi-armed bandit — usually a Bayesian (Thompson-sampling) bandit — with the candidate and incumbent as arms. A per-request reward signal (binary thumbs-up, task completion, downstream metric) updates each arm's posterior reward distribution, and allocation grows toward the arm with the higher sustained posterior. Below the traffic volume a bandit needs to accumulate signal, fall back to a fixed A/B split or canary-with-manual-cutover.

**Auto-halt and regression capture.** Any exit-criterion violation halts promotion. The bandit also self-corrects: if the candidate's posterior reward falls below the incumbent's with confidence, allocation naturally drains away from it; pair this with an explicit rollback threshold to fully demote the build. Crucially, regressions are first-class outputs — the failing traces are bundled and folded into the regression eval set, so each failure permanently hardens the test harness.

The unit of promotion is an individual change (a prompt, a model swap, a retrieval tweak), which is what distinguishes this from a per-action-type autonomy ladder.
methodologies.intent: Move an agent change through stages that widen exposure as results hold up. Run it in shadow, then on a small canary slice, then let traffic shift toward the better version. A drop in the numbers stops the rollout on its own.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this for any live agent or LLM app where changes ship often and a bad change can hurt the user experience or cost. It works best when traffic is high, because the small canary slice needs enough volume to catch problems. Don't apply the traffic-shifting stage on low-volume systems, where the learning method (a multi-armed bandit) cannot gather enough signal. There, fall back to a plain shadow run plus a manual switch.

inputs.name: Production traffic stream
inputs.description: Live requests you can copy to the new version for a shadow run and split for the canary slice.

inputs.name: Reward signal
inputs.description: A result you can measure on each request, such as a user thumbs-up, a completed task, or a downstream metric. The traffic-shifting stage uses this to decide which version to favor.

inputs.name: Regression detector
inputs.description: Automatic checks that flag when a new version's reward or error rate falls below the current one.

outputs.name: Promotion log
outputs.description: A record you can audit. It shows which change passed which stage and on what evidence.

outputs.name: Regression trace bundle
outputs.description: The failing cases saved from any stage that did not pass, ready for offline debugging.

outputs.name: Traffic allocation policy
outputs.description: The current rule for how much traffic each version gets. This is the bandit's belief or the A/B split.

steps.name: Shadow the new build
steps.description: Copy production traffic to the new version. Compare its outputs and metrics offline. No user is affected. This catches obvious crashes, slower responses, and large output drift before any user sees the change.

steps.name: Define the canary slice and exit criteria
steps.description: Pick a small live slice, usually 1 to 5 percent. Write down what 'pass' means before you open it: the minimum sample size, the regression allowed on each metric, and the time window. Freeze these rules first.

steps.name: Open the canary
steps.description: Send the small slice to the new version at full risk. Watch the error rate, the latency, the reward signal, and how often guardrails trip.

steps.name: Collect regression traces on any failure
steps.description: If the canary misses any of its pass rules, stop the rollout and save the failing cases in a bundle. That bundle becomes a new test for the next round.

steps.name: Promote to bandit or A/B
steps.description: When the canary clears its bar, hand traffic control to a learning method that shifts traffic toward the better version (a Bayesian bandit), or a simple fixed A/B split. It gives the new version more traffic as its results pull ahead.

steps.name: Watch the results and auto-rollback on regression
steps.description: Keep watching the bandit's view of each version. If the new version's reward clearly drops below the current one, the bandit moves traffic away from it on its own. Add a firm rollback threshold to fully retire the build.

principles: Exposure widens only on evidence. Every stage gates the next.
A regression is a useful output. Save it as traces and fold it into the test set. Do not just roll back.
A learning method (bandit) replaces a fixed A/B split once traffic is high enough. Below that, A/B or a canary with a manual switch is the honest choice.
Every stage has a pass rule. Freeze it before the stage opens.

known_failure_modes.note: Skipping shadow and canary because the new build 'looked great in dev' — the cliff is exactly what shadow is for.
Stage gates set so high that no change ever advances past canary, freezing the system in indefinite trial mode.
Failing canaries quietly rolled back without capturing the regression trace — the same failure ships again next quarter.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: Monitoring patterns including 'Shadow Mode' and 'Canary Deployments' ... 'Regression Trace Collection' ... Experimentation approaches: 'Shadow Deployments,' 'A/B Testing,' and 'Bayesian Bandits'

sources.title: LLM Canary Prompting in Production: Shadow Tests, Drift Alarms, and Safe Rollouts
sources.quote: Treat prompt changes like deployments, and wrap them in the same safety rails you'd give any other production rollout.

methodologies.name: Five-Level Agent Progression
methodologies.summary: A six-step ladder for how capable an AI agent is. The steps run from Level 0, fully manual, up to Level 5, fully autonomous, with rule-based, automated, and workflow stages in between. A team picks a target step based on how risky the task is and how much value it brings. To climb a step, the lower step's safety checks must be in place and the agent must pass its tests. This rates the agent as a whole, not one action at a time. Teams use it to agree on what 'done' means before they start building.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> L0: Manual baseline
  L0 --> L1: rules + automation in place
  L1 --> L2: ML triggers added (IPA)
  L2 --> L3: agentic workflow with planning
  L3 --> L4: semi-autonomous with HITL gates
  L4 --> L5: fully autonomous, evals + guardrails proven over months
  L5 --> L4: regression demotes one step pending fix
  L4 --> L3: regression demotes one step pending fix
  L3 --> L2: regression demotes one step pending fix
  note right of L3
    Each promotion requires:
    - prior-level guardrails in place
    - eval set passing at threshold
    - documented evidence
  end note
  note right of L4
    Skipping steps (e.g. L1->L4)
    ships demos that break
    on production drift
  end note

methodologies.example_scenario:
An insurance carrier is scoping an agent for claims processing across three task families: first-notice-of-loss intake (high volume, low risk per call), document-completeness checks (medium volume, medium risk — wrong call delays payouts), and settlement-amount calculation (low volume, high risk — wrong call costs real money or under-pays customers). The CTO wants L5 'fully autonomous claims agent'. The chief actuary wants L1 'rule-based with human review on everything'. The framework is used to align them.

They map each task family to a candidate level: FNOL intake at L3 (agentic workflow, since multimodal documents and unstructured speech require reasoning), document-completeness at L2 (IPA — ML triggers on existing OCR pipeline), settlement calculation at L1 (rule-based — actuarial tables don't need an agent, and regulators won't accept opaque reasoning). The CTO and actuary both sign off; the target is explicitly mixed across tasks rather than one number for the whole agent.

For FNOL intake — the most ambitious — they list the L2 preconditions: OCR pipeline at 98% field-extraction accuracy on the existing form set, exception-routing rules in place, observability into every call. The L3 advancement criteria are: planner-eval passing at the threshold the agent-platform team published, and 60 days of L2 operating with the published exception rate. Construction proceeds one level at a time. Six months in, the team reviews and confirms FNOL has reached L3 with the named evidence; the actuary asks whether settlement could move to L2 given new explainability tools, and they re-open that question. The ladder is a planning instrument, revisited as portfolio risk shifts, not a static org chart.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The ladder has six steps:

- **Level 0 — Manual:** no automation; a human does the task.
- **Level 1 — Rule-Based:** fixed-rule automation with no learning.
- **Level 2 — Intelligent Process Automation (IPA):** machine-learning triggers bolted onto an existing automated pipeline (e.g. ML classification on an OCR feed).
- **Level 3 — Agentic Workflows:** an agent that plans and reasons over multi-step, often multimodal or unstructured input.
- **Level 4 — Semi-Autonomous:** the agent acts, but with human-in-the-loop gates on consequential decisions.
- **Level 5 — Fully Autonomous:** the agent operates unsupervised, justified only after evals and guardrails have held over months.

**Selection and gating.** The target step is chosen by task risk and business value, not by technical ambition. Targets are typically *mixed across a task portfolio* — one agent can be L4 for FAQ and L2 for refunds. Advancement is gated: each promotion requires the prior level's guardrails already in place, the eval set passing at a published threshold, and documented evidence (e.g. L4→L5 requires months of logged human-in-the-loop acceptance at threshold).

**Build discipline.** Advance one step at a time. Skipping steps (L1→L4) ships demos that collapse on first production drift, because the eval at the destination level was never built.

This is a planning and stakeholder-alignment instrument used *before* construction and revisited as portfolio risk shifts — distinct from a per-action-type runtime promotion gate (crawl-walk-run), which it should be paired with at runtime.
methodologies.intent: Place an agent on a six-step capability ladder. This makes the target level of independence, and the safety checks needed to reach it, clear before anyone builds.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this early, when you are scoping an agent project. It helps most when people disagree on how independent the system should be, or when you are planning a roadmap that spans several quarters. It also works as a look-back tool: 'we said L4, we shipped L2.5'. Don't apply it once building is underway and the per-action gate (crawl-walk-run) is the right tool. The ladder is for planning, not for runtime control.

inputs.name: Task portfolio
inputs.description: The set of tasks the agent will handle. Each task is marked with its risk and its value.

inputs.name: Stakeholder constraints
inputs.description: Limits on how independent the agent can be. These come from regulators, the organisation, and how much risk people will accept.

inputs.name: Prior-level evidence
inputs.description: Test results and past incidents from the level the system runs at today.

outputs.name: Target level declaration
outputs.description: The target step on the L0 to L5 ladder, set per task or per system, with the reason for it.

outputs.name: Advancement criteria
outputs.description: The named safety checks, tests, and live signals required to climb one step.

outputs.name: Capability roadmap
outputs.description: A step-by-step plan to move from the current step to the target step.

steps.name: Map tasks to candidate levels
steps.description: For each task, find its natural step. The steps are manual (L0), rule-based (L1), ML triggers on existing automation (L2), an agent that plans (L3), semi-autonomous with human gates (L4), and fully autonomous (L5). Let the task's risk and value drive the choice, not technical excitement.

steps.name: Declare a target level per task or system
steps.description: Write down the target. Get explicit sign-off from stakeholders. Mixed targets are normal. A support agent may be L4 for FAQ and L2 for refunds.

steps.name: Define prior-level guardrails as preconditions
steps.description: For each step, list the safety checks and tests that must already pass at the step below before you consider climbing. Moving from L4 to L5 usually requires months of logged human acceptance at the published bar.

steps.name: Build to one level above current
steps.description: Climb one step at a time. Skipping steps, such as jumping from L1 to L4, tends to ship demos that break once real traffic shifts.

steps.name: Gate advancement on evidence
steps.description: Climb only when the named safety checks are in place and the test set passes the bar. Write down the evidence. If the numbers drop at a higher step, the system moves down one step until it is fixed.

steps.name: Revisit target level on portfolio shifts
steps.description: When a task's risk or value changes, such as a new regulation or a new product line, set the target step again. The ladder is for planning, not a permanent label.

principles: The autonomy level is a planning choice. It is driven by task risk and value, not by technical capability alone.
Climb one step at a time. Skipping steps ships demos that production breaks.
Higher steps need the lower steps' safety checks already in place.
The ladder is for aligning stakeholders, not for runtime control. Pair it with per-action gating at runtime.

known_failure_modes.note: Declaring L5 as the target on day one because the demo looked autonomous — guardrails for L1–L4 never get built.
Skipping steps collapses on first production drift; the eval at the destination level never existed.
Never advancing past L3 because no team owns building the next-level guardrails.

sources.title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence: Harnessing AI Agents to Reinvent Business, Work, and Life
sources.chapter: Ch 3 'The Five Levels of AI Agents: From Automation to Autonomy' (pp. 61–81)
sources.quote: The Five Levels Of AI Agents: From Automation To Autonomy (pages 61-81)

sources.title: Truly Useful AI: Reviewing Pascal Bornet's Five-Level Guide to Getting Stuff Done with Agents
sources.quote: Level 0 (Manual) ... Level 1 (Rule-Based): Basic automation following fixed rules ... Level 2 (Intelligent Process Automation) ... Level 3 (Agentic Workflows) ... Level 4 (Semi-Autonomous) ... Level 5 (Fully Autonomous)
